title: Diabetes Mellitus: Diagnosis, Classification, and Pathophysiology
authors: Alvin C. Powers, Kevin D. Niswender, Carmella Evans-Molina

items.title: Diagnostic thresholds for diabetes and prediabetes
items.content:
Diabetes (any ONE, confirmed on a separate day unless unequivocal hyperglycemia with acute metabolic decompensation):
• HbA1c ≥ 6.5% (≥48 mmol/mol)
• Fasting plasma glucose (FPG) ≥ 7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL) — fasting = no caloric intake ≥8 h
• 2-h plasma glucose ≥ 11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) on 75-g OGTT
• Random plasma glucose ≥ 11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) PLUS classic symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss)

Prediabetes: HbA1c 5.7–6.4%, IFG (FPG 100–125 mg/dL), or IGT (2-h glucose 140–199 mg/dL).

Normal: HbA1c <5.7%, FPG <100 mg/dL, 2-h glucose <140 mg/dL.
keyPoints: A random glucose ≥200 mg/dL counts ONLY with classic symptoms.
Confirm abnormal screening on a separate day unless markedly elevated glucose or acute metabolic decompensation.
WHO defines IFG as FPG >110 mg/dL; ADA uses 100 mg/dL.
Glucose values are continuous — risk rises continuously, not at discrete cut-points.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "An HbA1c ≥6.5% (>48 mmol/mol), an FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL), or a glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) 2 h after an oral glucose challenge meets the criteria for the diagnosis of DM. A random plasma glucose concentration ≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) accompanied by classic symptoms of DM ... is also sufficient."

items.title: Type 1 vs Type 2 vs other diabetes
items.content:
Type 1 DM: autoimmune destruction of beta cells → absolute insulin deficiency. 25–50% present with DKA. Up to 40% have onset after age 30 and are often misdiagnosed as type 2.

Type 2 DM: heterogeneous — variable insulin resistance, impaired insulin secretion, and increased hepatic glucose production. ≥80% of patients are obese (especially visceral).

Other: Monogenic (MODY — autosomal dominant, onset usually <25 y, impaired secretion); diseases of exocrine pancreas (pancreatitis, CF, hemochromatosis); endocrinopathies (acromegaly, Cushing's, glucagonoma, pheochromocytoma); drug/chemical-induced (glucocorticoids, calcineurin/mTOR inhibitors, atypical antipsychotics, thiazides); gestational DM.
keyPoints: Adult-onset type 1 is common — do not assume an adult with new diabetes has type 2.
MODY: think autosomal-dominant family history, young, lean, non-ketotic.
Ketosis-prone (atypical) diabetes — present with DKA but do NOT need long-term insulin; often African American or Asian.
items.reference: CLASSIFICATION & Table 415-1: "Type 1 DM develops as a result of autoimmunity against the insulin-producing beta cells... Type 2 DM is a heterogeneous group of disorders characterized by variable degrees of insulin resistance, impaired insulin secretion, and increased hepatic glucose production."

items.title: Who and when to screen for type 2 diabetes
items.content:
• Screen all adults from age 35, repeat every 3 years.
• Screen earlier/any age if overweight/obese (BMI ≥25; ≥23 in Asian Americans) PLUS a risk factor: family history, high-risk race/ethnicity, hypertension (≥130/80), HDL <35 and/or TG >250, PCOS or acanthosis nigricans, CVD, physical inactivity.
• Prior IFG/IGT/HbA1c 5.7–6.4%: screen annually.
• Prior GDM: screen at least every 3 years (lifelong).
• Cystic fibrosis: OGTT from age 10. Post-transplant: OGTT.
• Use OGTT (not HbA1c) for CF-related and post-transplant diabetes.
keyPoints: HbA1c and FPG are the preferred screening tests in asymptomatic adults.
Type 2 DM may be present for up to a decade before diagnosis — some already have complications at diagnosis.
CF-related and post-transplant diabetes need OGTT, because HbA1c is unreliable in these settings.
items.reference: SCREENING & Table 415-2: "The ADA recommends screening all individuals aged >35 years every 3 years and screening individuals at an earlier age if they are overweight and have one additional risk factor... Screening for cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (beginning at age 10 years) and posttransplantation diabetes should use OGTT rather than HbA1c."

items.title: Islet autoantibodies and the 3-stage model of type 1 DM
items.content:
Islet cell autoantibodies (ICAs) target insulin, GAD, IA-2, and ZnT8. Present in >85% of new-onset type 1 DM.

Staging:
• Stage 1 — ≥2 autoantibodies, normoglycemia.
• Stage 2 — autoantibodies + dysglycemia (still asymptomatic).
• Stage 3 — clinical hyperglycemia/symptoms.

In high-genetic-risk children, ≥2 ICAs carry ~70% risk of type 1 DM at 10 y and >80% at 15 y. Beta-cell destruction is mainly CD8+ T-cell mediated; ICAs are markers, not the direct cause.

Teplizumab (anti-CD3 mAb): a single 14-day course delays onset of stage 3 by a median of 32.5 months in stage 2 patients — FDA approved.
keyPoints: ≥2 islet autoantibodies = stage 1 type 1 DM (even with normal glucose).
Measure ICAs in adults with type-1 features (young age, DKA, rapid insulin need) to detect adult-onset type 1.
Teplizumab is the first disease-modifying therapy that delays clinical type 1 DM.
items.reference: PATHOGENESIS — TYPE 1 DM: "The presence of two or more autoantibodies is now designated as stage 1 type 1 DM... a single 14-day course of teplizumab... delayed the onset of stage 3 type 1 DM in high-risk individuals with multiple autoantibodies and dysglycemia (i.e., stage 2 type 1 DM) by a median of 32.5 months."

items.title: Why fasting vs postprandial hyperglycemia occur in type 2 DM
items.content:
Type 2 DM combines insulin resistance, impaired (especially first-phase) insulin secretion, and excessive hepatic glucose production. Most data support insulin resistance preceding the secretory defect; overt diabetes appears only when secretion can no longer compensate.

• Increased hepatic glucose output → predominantly raises FASTING plasma glucose.
• Decreased peripheral (skeletal muscle) glucose uptake → predominantly POSTPRANDIAL hyperglycemia.

Dyslipidemia of type 2 DM: ↑ triglycerides, ↓ HDL, and increased small dense LDL — driven by increased free fatty acid flux and hepatic VLDL output. 'Glucose toxicity' and 'lipotoxicity' further worsen beta-cell function (and improve with better control).
keyPoints: Fasting hyperglycemia ≈ liver (gluconeogenesis); postprandial hyperglycemia ≈ muscle uptake.
Beta-cell function is already reduced ~50% at the onset of type 2 DM.
Improving glycemic control improves beta-cell function (reversible glucose toxicity).
items.reference: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY — TYPE 2 DM: "Increased hepatic glucose output predominantly accounts for increased FPG levels, whereas decreased peripheral glucose utilization results in postprandial hyperglycemia."

items.title: Gestational diabetes — diagnosis timing and follow-up
items.content:
GDM = glucose intolerance developing in the 2nd or 3rd trimester. Diabetes diagnosed in the FIRST trimester is classified as preexisting (pregestational) diabetes, not GDM.

• 35–60% of women with GDM develop diabetes within 10–20 years.
• Offspring have increased risk of metabolic syndrome and type 2 DM.
• Screen women with prior GDM at least every 3 years, lifelong.
keyPoints: First-trimester hyperglycemia = likely preexisting diabetes, not GDM.
GDM confers high long-term maternal risk of type 2 DM — needs lifelong surveillance.
items.reference: GESTATIONAL DM: "diabetes diagnosed within the first trimester be classified as preexisting pregestational diabetes rather than GDM... Most women with GDM revert to normal glucose tolerance postpartum but have a substantial risk (35–60%) of developing DM in the next 10–20 years."

items.question: An asymptomatic 52-year-old man has a single fasting plasma glucose of 134 mg/dL on routine screening. What is the most appropriate next step to establish a diagnosis of diabetes?
options: Diagnose diabetes immediately and start metformin
Repeat a confirmatory test (FPG or HbA1c) on a separate day
Order an immediate 75-g oral glucose tolerance test that day
Reassure him that this is within the prediabetes range
items.explanation: In an asymptomatic patient without markedly elevated glucose or acute metabolic decompensation, an abnormal screening result must be confirmed by repeat testing on a different day before diagnosing diabetes. A FPG of 134 mg/dL (≥126) is in the diabetes range but a single value is insufficient. Immediate diagnosis/treatment is reserved for unequivocal hyperglycemia with decompensation.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "abnormalities on screening tests for diabetes should be repeated before making a definitive diagnosis of DM, unless acute metabolic derangements or a markedly elevated plasma glucose are present."

items.question: Which single set of values correctly meets the diagnostic threshold for diabetes mellitus?
items.explanation: Diabetes thresholds are HbA1c ≥6.5%, FPG ≥126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L), or 2-h OGTT ≥200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L). Option B meets all three. The other options fall in the prediabetes (5.7–6.4% / IFG 100–125 / IGT 140–199) or normal ranges.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "An HbA1c ≥6.5%... an FPG ≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL), or a glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) 2 h after an oral glucose challenge meets the criteria for the diagnosis of DM."

items.question: A 60-year-old woman reports polyuria, polydipsia, and 4 kg weight loss. A random (non-fasting) plasma glucose is 240 mg/dL. Which statement is correct?
options: A second confirmatory test is mandatory before any treatment
This meets diagnostic criteria for diabetes because classic symptoms accompany a random glucose ≥200 mg/dL
A random glucose can never be used to diagnose diabetes
She has impaired glucose tolerance, not diabetes
items.explanation: A random plasma glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) together with classic symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) is sufficient to diagnose diabetes. The symptomatic, markedly hyperglycemic presentation does not require a separate confirmatory test.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "A random plasma glucose concentration ≥11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) accompanied by classic symptoms of DM (e.g., polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) is also sufficient for the diagnosis of DM."

items.question: In which clinical situation is HbA1c LEAST reliable for diagnosing diabetes, so that an oral glucose tolerance test should be used instead?
options: A 10-year-old with cystic fibrosis being screened for CF-related diabetes
A 38-year-old woman with a family history of type 2 DM
items.explanation: OGTT (not HbA1c) is recommended for screening cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (from age 10) and post-transplant diabetes. Hemoglobinopathies, altered red-cell turnover, and ancestry can also distort HbA1c. The other patients are appropriately screened with HbA1c or FPG.
items.reference: SCREENING: "Screening for cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (beginning at age 10 years) and posttransplantation diabetes should use OGTT rather than HbA1c." Also DIAGNOSIS: "some clinical conditions (e.g., hemoglobinopathies) and ancestry may influence the HbA1c."

items.question: According to ADA criteria, which overweight adult should be screened for type 2 diabetes at an age earlier than 35?
options: BMI 27 with no other risk factors
BMI 26 with hypertension (BP 138/86) and a sibling with type 2 DM
items.explanation: Earlier screening is indicated for adults who are overweight/obese (BMI ≥25; ≥23 in Asian Americans) AND have ≥1 additional risk factor — here hypertension and a first-degree relative with type 2 DM. Overweight alone without a risk factor, or a normal BMI without risk factors, does not trigger earlier-than-35 screening.
items.reference: Table 415-2: "Consider testing in adults of any age who are overweight or obese (BMI ≥25 kg/m² or ≥23 in Asian Americans) who have one of these risk factors: family history of diabetes... hypertension (blood pressure ≥130/80 mmHg)..."

items.question: A lean 32-year-old presents with new hyperglycemia and DKA and is labeled 'type 2 diabetes.' Which test best clarifies that this is actually type 1 DM?
options: Islet cell autoantibodies (GAD, IA-2, ZnT8, insulin)
items.explanation: Up to 40% of type 1 DM has onset after age 30 and is frequently misdiagnosed as type 2. Islet autoantibodies (GAD, IA-2, ZnT8, anti-insulin) are present in >85% of new-onset type 1 DM and are recommended when an adult has type-1 features (younger age, ketoacidosis, rapid insulin need). HbA1c and random glucose confirm hyperglycemia but do not distinguish the type.
items.reference: PATHOGENESIS — TYPE 1 DM: "Measurement of ICAs is recommended for adults who have phenotypic features similar to those in classic type 1 DM (e.g., younger age at diagnosis, ketoacidosis, or rapid need for insulin treatment)."

items.question: A 12-year-old sibling of a child with type 1 DM is found to have two islet autoantibodies plus dysglycemia, but no symptoms. Which intervention has been shown to delay progression to clinical (stage 3) type 1 diabetes?
items.explanation: This child has stage 2 type 1 DM (≥2 autoantibodies + dysglycemia). A single 14-day course of teplizumab (Fc-receptor-nonbinding anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody) delayed onset of stage 3 type 1 DM by a median of 32.5 months and is FDA approved for stage 2 disease. Insulin, metformin, and sulfonylureas do not delay autoimmune progression.
items.reference: PATHOGENESIS — TYPE 1 DM: "a single 14-day course of teplizumab, an Fc receptor-nonbinding anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody, which delayed the onset of stage 3 type 1 DM in high-risk individuals with multiple autoantibodies and dysglycemia (i.e., stage 2 type 1 DM) by a median of 32.5 months. This treatment, recently approved by the U.S. FDA for stage 2 type 1 DM..."

items.question: Parents with type 1 DM ask about the risk to their children. Which counseling statement is most accurate?
options: Risk to a child of an affected parent is about 50%
Risk to a child is relatively low (≈1–9%); most people with type 1 DM have no affected relative
If one identical twin has type 1 DM, the other is essentially certain to develop it
Type 1 DM has no genetic component
items.explanation: The risk of type 1 DM in a child of an affected parent is only ~1–9%, and ~6–7% in a sibling; >80% of people with type 1 DM have no affected first-degree relative. Identical-twin concordance is only 30–70% (not near-certain), showing strong non-genetic/environmental contributions. HLA on chromosome 6 accounts for ~50% of genetic risk.
items.reference: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS: "the risk is relatively low: 1–9% if the parent has type 1 DM and 6–7% in a sibling... the majority of individuals with type 1 DM (>80%) do not have a relative with this disorder." / "concordance of type 1 DM in identical twins ranges from 30–70%."

items.question: In type 2 diabetes, increased hepatic glucose production is most directly responsible for which abnormality?
items.explanation: Increased hepatic glucose output (failure of insulin to suppress gluconeogenesis) predominantly raises the fasting plasma glucose, whereas decreased peripheral (muscle) glucose uptake drives postprandial hyperglycemia. Type 2 DM dyslipidemia includes HIGH triglycerides, not low.
items.reference: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY — TYPE 2 DM: "Increased hepatic glucose output predominantly accounts for increased FPG levels, whereas decreased peripheral glucose utilization results in postprandial hyperglycemia."

items.question: A patient on long-term high-dose glucocorticoids develops new hyperglycemia. This is best classified as which category of diabetes?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids are a classic cause of drug-induced diabetes (an 'other specific type'). Other culprits include calcineurin and mTOR inhibitors (post-transplant), atypical antipsychotics, thiazides, statins, and protease inhibitors. Recognizing a secondary, potentially modifiable cause changes management.
items.reference: Table 415-1 (III.G): "Drug- or chemical-induced—glucocorticoids, calcineurin and mTOR inhibitors (after organ transplantation)... antipsychotics (second-generation atypicals and others)... thiazides..."

items.question: Which endocrine disorder is a recognized secondary cause of hyperglycemia/diabetes by antagonizing insulin action?
items.explanation: Counter-regulatory hormone excess states cause secondary diabetes: acromegaly, Cushing's syndrome, glucagonoma, pheochromocytoma, hyperthyroidism, somatostatinoma, and aldosteronoma. Screening for and treating the underlying endocrinopathy can improve glycemia.
items.reference: Table 415-1 (III.F): "Endocrinopathies—acromegaly, Cushing's syndrome, glucagonoma, pheochromocytoma, hyperthyroidism, somatostatinoma, aldosteronoma."

items.question: A non-obese 22-year-old has mild stable hyperglycemia and a striking autosomal-dominant family history of young-onset diabetes across three generations. Which diagnosis should be considered?
items.explanation: MODY (monogenic diabetes) is characterized by autosomal-dominant inheritance, onset usually <25 years, and impaired insulin secretion, typically in lean, non-ketotic patients. Recognizing MODY matters because some subtypes (e.g., HNF1A) respond to sulfonylureas and others (glucokinase) need no treatment.
items.reference: OTHER TYPES OF DM: "Monogenic diabetes, sometimes referred to as maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY), are types of DM characterized by autosomal dominant inheritance, early onset of hyperglycemia (usually <25 years...), and impaired insulin secretion."

items.question: A woman is found to have diabetes-range hyperglycemia at her first prenatal visit in the first trimester. How should this be classified?
items.explanation: The ADA classifies diabetes detected in the first trimester as preexisting (pregestational) diabetes, not GDM. GDM by definition develops in the 2nd or 3rd trimester. This distinction affects management and risk assessment for the pregnancy.
items.reference: GESTATIONAL DM: "The American Diabetes Association (ADA) recommends that diabetes diagnosed within the first trimester be classified as preexisting pregestational diabetes rather than GDM."

items.question: A woman had GDM that resolved after delivery. What long-term follow-up is recommended?
options: No further screening is needed once glucose normalizes postpartum
Lifelong screening for diabetes/prediabetes at least every 3 years
items.explanation: Women with prior GDM have a 35–60% risk of developing diabetes over 10–20 years, so the ADA recommends lifelong screening at least every 3 years. Routine indefinite home monitoring or empiric insulin is not indicated after resolution.
items.reference: GESTATIONAL DM: "the ADA recommends that women with a history of GDM undergo lifelong screening for the development of diabetes or prediabetes at least every 3 years."

items.question: A patient has an HbA1c of 6.1%. Which statement best guides management?
options: This is prediabetes; counsel on lifestyle change and cardiovascular risk reduction, and rescreen
This is normal; no action needed
This requires immediate OGTT to exclude type 1 DM
items.explanation: HbA1c 5.7–6.4% defines prediabetes. These patients are at higher risk of progressing to type 2 DM and of cardiovascular disease and should be counseled on risk reduction. Notably, the 5-year risk of progression is ~25.5% when HbA1c is 6.0–6.5%, making lifestyle intervention important.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "The term prediabetes refers to those with an HbA1c of 5.7–6.4%, IFG, or IGT... individuals in all three groups are at greater risk of progressing to type 2 DM, have an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, and should be counseled about ways to decrease these risks."

items.question: C-peptide measurement is clinically useful primarily because it:
options: Directly causes glucose uptake in muscle
Is a marker of endogenous insulin secretion and distinguishes endogenous from exogenous insulin
Is the active hormone replaced in insulin therapy
Confirms the presence of islet autoantibodies
items.explanation: C-peptide is co-secreted with insulin in equimolar amounts and cleared more slowly, so it is a useful marker of endogenous insulin secretion. It helps discriminate endogenous from exogenous (injected) insulin — central to the evaluation of hypoglycemia (e.g., insulinoma vs surreptitious insulin use).
items.reference: INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS: "Because C-peptide is cleared more slowly than insulin, it is a useful marker of insulin secretion and allows discrimination of endogenous and exogenous sources of insulin in the evaluation of hypoglycemia."

items.question: In the United States, diabetes mellitus is the leading cause of all of the following EXCEPT:
items.explanation: DM is the leading cause of end-stage renal disease, nontraumatic lower-extremity amputations, and adult blindness in the U.S. Cardiovascular disease is the main cause of morbidity and mortality in people with diabetes. Pneumonia is not in this list. This burden underpins aggressive screening and risk-factor control.
items.reference: Opening paragraph: "In the United States, DM is the leading cause of end-stage renal disease (ESRD), nontraumatic lower-extremity amputations, and adult blindness. Persons with diabetes are at increased risk for cardiovascular disease, which is the main cause of morbidity and mortality."

items.question: Which is the rate-limiting (glucose-sensing) step controlling glucose-stimulated insulin secretion in the beta cell?
options: Closure of the ATP-sensitive K+ channel
items.explanation: Glucose entering the beta cell is phosphorylated by glucokinase — the rate-limiting step that controls glucose-regulated insulin secretion (the 'glucose sensor'). Downstream, ATP closes the K+ channel (the sulfonylurea/Kir6.2 channel), depolarizing the cell and opening Ca2+ channels to trigger insulin release. This explains why glucokinase mutations cause MODY.
items.reference: INSULIN SECRETION: "Glucose phosphorylation by glucokinase is the rate-limiting step that controls glucose-regulated insulin secretion."

items.question: Incretin hormones (GLP-1 and GIP) stimulate insulin secretion in a way that is therapeutically advantageous because they:
options: Stimulate insulin regardless of glucose level, risking hypoglycemia
Stimulate insulin only when blood glucose is above the fasting level, and also suppress glucagon
Act only in the fasting state
items.explanation: GLP-1 and GIP enhance glucose-stimulated insulin secretion only when blood glucose is above the fasting level, and they suppress glucagon. This glucose-dependence underlies the low intrinsic hypoglycemia risk of incretin-based therapies (GLP-1 receptor agonists, DPP-4 inhibitors) used in type 2 DM.
items.reference: INSULIN SECRETION: "GLP-1... and GIP are incretin hormones that bind specific receptors on the beta cell to stimulate insulin secretion... but they have this effect only when the blood glucose is above the fasting level. Incretin hormones also suppress glucagon production and secretion."

items.question: Which lipid pattern is characteristic of the dyslipidemia of type 2 diabetes?
options: Low triglycerides, high HDL, large buoyant LDL
High triglycerides, low HDL, increased small dense LDL
Normal triglycerides with very high HDL
Isolated severe hypercholesterolemia with normal triglycerides
items.explanation: Type 2 DM dyslipidemia is characterized by elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL, and increased small dense LDL particles, driven by increased free-fatty-acid flux and hepatic VLDL-triglyceride output. This atherogenic profile contributes to the high cardiovascular risk in type 2 DM.
items.reference: INCREASED HEPATIC GLUCOSE AND LIPID PRODUCTION: "This is also responsible for the dyslipidemia found in type 2 DM (elevated triglycerides, reduced high-density lipoprotein [HDL], and increased small dense low-density lipoprotein [LDL] particles)."

items.question: A patient presents with diabetic ketoacidosis but is subsequently maintained off insulin without recurrent ketosis. This presentation is best described as:
options: Classic type 1 DM requiring lifelong insulin
MODY due to a glucokinase mutation
items.explanation: Ketosis-prone diabetes is an atypical form in which patients present with ketoacidosis yet do not require long-term exogenous insulin. Many such individuals are of African American or Asian heritage. Recognizing it prevents committing a patient to unnecessary lifelong insulin.
items.reference: ATYPICAL DIABETES: "An additional example is ketosis-prone diabetes, where individuals present with ketoacidosis but do not require long-term exogenous insulin therapy. Many of these individuals are African American or Asian in heritage."

items.question: Which statement about the heritability of type 2 diabetes is correct and clinically useful?
options: If both parents have type 2 DM, offspring risk approaches 70%
Type 2 DM is a single-gene disorder
Known genetic loci can reliably predict who will develop type 2 DM
items.explanation: Type 2 DM has a strong genetic component: identical-twin concordance is 70–90%, and if both parents are affected the offspring risk approaches 70%. However, it is polygenic/multifactorial, and currently identified loci cannot reliably predict individual risk — so family history, not genotyping, guides screening.
items.reference: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (Type 2): "The concordance of type 2 DM in identical twins is between 70 and 90%... if both parents have type 2 DM, the risk approaches 70%... it is currently not possible to use a combination of known genetic loci to reliably predict type 2 DM."

items.question: A patient with poorly controlled type 2 DM improves beta-cell function after intensified glycemic control. This best illustrates which concept?
items.explanation: Chronic hyperglycemia paradoxically impairs islet function ('glucose toxicity'); improving glycemic control reverses some of this and improves beta-cell function — an important clinical reason to lower glucose promptly. Lipotoxicity from elevated free fatty acids acts similarly.
items.reference: IMPAIRED INSULIN SECRETION: "chronic hyperglycemia paradoxically impairs islet function ('glucose toxicity')... Improvement in glycemic control is often associated with improved islet function, an important clinical consideration."

items.question: What is the correct glucose load and a principal current clinical use of the oral glucose tolerance test?
options: 50 g load; used routinely to monitor all diabetics
75 g anhydrous glucose; used mainly in pregnancy and screening for CF-related diabetes
100 g load; the preferred test for asymptomatic adults
25 g load; used to diagnose type 1 DM
items.explanation: The OGTT uses 75 g of anhydrous glucose. It is not used routinely; HbA1c and FPG are preferred in asymptomatic adults. The OGTT is retained mainly for pregnancy/GDM screening and for cystic-fibrosis-related diabetes screening.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "An oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) is not often used in routine clinical care with the exception of pregnancy care and screening for gestational diabetes or cystic fibrosis-related diabetes." / Fig. 415-1: "a glucose load containing the equivalent of 75 g of anhydrous glucose."

items.question: Which feature is most consistent with new-onset type 1 (rather than type 2) diabetes?
options: Presentation with diabetic ketoacidosis in 25–50% of patients
Strong association with central obesity in nearly all patients
Insulin resistance as the primary defect
items.explanation: Type 1 DM results from autoimmune beta-cell destruction with insulin deficiency; 25–50% present with DKA. It can occur at any age (up to ~40% onset after 30). Central obesity and primary insulin resistance characterize type 2, not type 1.
items.reference: PATHOGENESIS — TYPE 1 DM: "Individuals with type 1 DM, especially children, often have dramatic and acute symptoms of diabetes with marked elevations in the blood glucose, with 25–50% presenting with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)."

items.question: Which patient with type 2 diabetes is most likely to achieve diabetes remission after bariatric surgery?
options: Long-standing insulin-requiring disease with very high HbA1c
Earlier-stage disease: shorter duration, not on insulin, lower HbA1c
Patient with a glucokinase MODY mutation
items.explanation: Patients with earlier-stage type 2 DM — no insulin requirement, shorter disease duration, and lower HbA1c — have better improvement after bariatric surgery. About 68% achieve initial remission within 5 years, though one-third relapse. Antibody-positive (type 1) and MODY patients are not candidates for diabetes remission via this route.
items.reference: Bariatric surgery section: "Patients with earlier-stage type 2 diabetes (i.e., those who do not need insulin, with shorter-duration disease, and with lower hemoglobin A1c) appear to have better improvement after bariatric surgery."

items.question: A patient who underwent Roux-en-Y gastric bypass for obesity/type 2 DM requires lifelong monitoring and supplementation for deficiencies of:
options: Vitamin B12, iron, folate, calcium, and vitamin D
No supplementation is needed after RYGB
items.explanation: Restrictive-malabsorptive procedures (e.g., RYGB) increase the risk of micronutrient deficiencies — vitamin B12, iron, folate, calcium, and vitamin D — and require lifelong supplementation. This is a key long-term management point after metabolic surgery.
items.reference: Bariatric surgery section: "the restrictive-malabsorptive procedures carry an increased risk for micronutrient deficiencies of vitamin B12, iron, folate, calcium, and vitamin D. Patients with restrictive-malabsorptive procedures require lifelong supplementation."

items.statement: A random plasma glucose of 210 mg/dL alone, in an asymptomatic patient, is sufficient to diagnose diabetes without any further testing.
items.explanation: False. A random glucose ≥200 mg/dL diagnoses diabetes only when accompanied by classic symptoms. In an asymptomatic patient the abnormal result must be confirmed by repeat testing on a separate day.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: random glucose ≥200 mg/dL is diagnostic only 'accompanied by classic symptoms'; otherwise 'abnormalities on screening tests... should be repeated before making a definitive diagnosis.'

items.statement: An HbA1c of 6.5% or greater meets the diagnostic threshold for diabetes mellitus.
items.explanation: True. HbA1c ≥6.5% (≥48 mmol/mol) is one of the accepted diagnostic criteria for diabetes, alongside FPG ≥126 mg/dL, 2-h OGTT ≥200 mg/dL, or random ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "An HbA1c ≥6.5% (>48 mmol/mol)... meets the criteria for the diagnosis of DM."

items.statement: Fasting for the FPG test is defined as no caloric intake for at least 8 hours.
items.explanation: True. Per the diagnostic figure, fasting is defined as no caloric intake for at least 8 hours.
items.reference: Fig. 415-1: "Fasting is defined as no caloric intake for at least 8 h."

items.statement: Type 1 diabetes can only present before puberty; an adult presenting with new diabetes must have type 2.
items.explanation: False. Type 1 DM can develop at any age — as many as 40% of cases have onset after age 30, and these adults are often misdiagnosed as type 2. Measuring islet autoantibodies clarifies the diagnosis.
items.reference: TYPE 1 DM: "recent data estimate that as many as 40% of individuals who develop type 1 DM have onset at over the age of 30. Many individuals with adult-onset type 1 DM are initially misdiagnosed with type 2 DM."

items.statement: The presence of two or more islet autoantibodies with normal glucose tolerance is now defined as stage 1 type 1 diabetes.
items.explanation: True. Detection of ≥2 islet autoantibodies (with normoglycemia) defines stage 1 type 1 DM; stage 2 adds dysglycemia; stage 3 is clinical disease.
items.reference: TYPE 1 DM: "the development of multiple autoantibodies is now defined as the onset of stage 1 type 1 DM."

items.statement: For cystic fibrosis-related diabetes, HbA1c is the preferred screening test.
items.explanation: False. CF-related diabetes (screen from age 10) and post-transplant diabetes should be screened with an OGTT rather than HbA1c, because HbA1c is unreliable in these settings.
items.reference: SCREENING: "Screening for cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (beginning at age 10 years) and posttransplantation diabetes should use OGTT rather than HbA1c."

items.statement: Women with a history of gestational diabetes should be screened for diabetes at least every 3 years for life.
items.explanation: True. Because 35–60% develop diabetes within 10–20 years, the ADA recommends lifelong screening at least every 3 years.
items.reference: GESTATIONAL DM: "women with a history of GDM undergo lifelong screening for the development of diabetes or prediabetes at least every 3 years."

items.statement: In most cases of type 2 diabetes, the insulin secretory defect appears before any insulin resistance.
items.explanation: False. Most studies support insulin resistance preceding the insulin secretory defect; overt diabetes develops only when insulin secretion becomes inadequate for the degree of resistance.
items.reference: PATHOGENESIS — TYPE 2 DM: "most studies support the view that insulin resistance precedes an insulin secretory defect but that diabetes develops only when insulin secretion becomes inadequate."

items.statement: Islet cell autoantibodies are the direct cause of beta-cell death in type 1 diabetes.
items.explanation: False. Islet autoantibodies are important biomarkers of risk but do not directly kill beta cells; destruction is mostly mediated by CD8+ T-cell cytotoxicity (with contributions from cytokines and reactive oxygen species).
items.reference: TYPE 1 DM Pathophysiology: "the antibodies do not have a direct role in beta cell death. Beta cell destruction is mostly likely mediated by direct CD8+ T cell-mediated cytotoxicity."

items.statement: Prediabetes carries an increased risk of cardiovascular disease in addition to the risk of progressing to type 2 diabetes.
items.explanation: True. Individuals with prediabetes (HbA1c 5.7–6.4%, IFG, or IGT) have increased risk both of progressing to type 2 DM and of cardiovascular disease, and should be counseled on risk reduction.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: prediabetes groups "are at greater risk of progressing to type 2 DM, have an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, and should be counseled about ways to decrease these risks."

items.statement: The WHO and ADA use identical fasting glucose cut-points to define impaired fasting glucose.
items.explanation: False. The ADA defines IFG as FPG 100–125 mg/dL (≥100), whereas the WHO defines IFG as FPG >110 mg/dL. The thresholds differ.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "IFG as defined as an FPG of 5.6–6.9 mmol/L (100–125 mg/dL)... The World Health Organization defines IFG as an FPG >6.1 mmol/L (110 mg/dL)."

items.statement: After bariatric surgery, diabetes remission is permanent in essentially all patients.
items.explanation: False. Although ~68% of patients achieve initial complete remission within 5 years, about one-third of those redevelop type 2 diabetes within 5 years. Remission is not guaranteed to be permanent.
items.reference: Bariatric surgery section: "68.2% of patients experienced an initial complete remission of type 2 diabetes within 5 years after surgery. However, among these patients, one-third redeveloped type 2 diabetes within 5 years."

items.assertion: In an asymptomatic patient, a single abnormal screening glucose should be confirmed on a separate day before diagnosing diabetes.
items.reason: The diagnosis of diabetes has major medical and financial implications, and glucose intolerance can sometimes revert to normal.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion: precisely because the diagnosis carries major implications (and can revert), confirmation on a separate day is required unless there is unequivocal hyperglycemia with decompensation.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "The diagnosis of DM has profound implications for an individual from both a medical and a financial standpoint. Thus, abnormalities on screening tests... should be repeated before making a definitive diagnosis."

items.assertion: Teplizumab is used to delay clinical type 1 diabetes in selected high-risk individuals.
items.reason: Teplizumab is an anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody that modulates the T-cell-mediated autoimmune destruction of beta cells.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Type 1 DM is driven by CD8+ T-cell-mediated beta-cell destruction; teplizumab (anti-CD3) interferes with this process and delayed stage 3 onset by a median of 32.5 months in stage 2 patients.
items.reference: TYPE 1 DM: teplizumab is "an Fc receptor-nonbinding anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody, which delayed the onset of stage 3 type 1 DM... by a median of 32.5 months"; destruction is "mediated by direct CD8+ T cell-mediated cytotoxicity."

items.assertion: Fasting hyperglycemia in type 2 diabetes is driven mainly by increased hepatic glucose production.
items.reason: Skeletal muscle is the principal site of insulin-stimulated postprandial glucose disposal.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion. Fasting hyperglycemia results from increased hepatic glucose output, whereas the muscle statement explains postprandial (not fasting) glucose handling — a true but separate fact.
items.reference: TYPE 2 DM: "Increased hepatic glucose output predominantly accounts for increased FPG levels, whereas decreased peripheral glucose utilization results in postprandial hyperglycemia."

items.assertion: Incretin-based therapies have a low intrinsic risk of hypoglycemia.
items.reason: GLP-1 and GIP augment insulin secretion only when blood glucose is above the fasting level.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion: because incretins stimulate insulin in a glucose-dependent manner (only above fasting glucose) and suppress glucagon, incretin-based drugs carry little intrinsic hypoglycemia risk.
items.reference: INSULIN SECRETION: incretins "stimulate insulin secretion... but they have this effect only when the blood glucose is above the fasting level. Incretin hormones also suppress glucagon production and secretion."

items.assertion: An adult presenting with diabetic ketoacidosis should not automatically be classified as type 2 diabetes.
items.reason: Type 1 diabetes can have its onset in adulthood and is frequently misdiagnosed as type 2.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because up to 40% of type 1 DM begins after age 30 and is often misdiagnosed as type 2, an adult with DKA warrants autoantibody testing rather than automatic type 2 labeling.
items.reference: TYPE 1 DM: "as many as 40% of individuals who develop type 1 DM have onset at over the age of 30. Many individuals with adult-onset type 1 DM are initially misdiagnosed with type 2 DM."

items.assertion: HbA1c may be unreliable for diagnosing diabetes in a patient with a hemoglobinopathy.
items.reason: Hemoglobinopathies and certain ancestries can influence the HbA1c result.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because hemoglobinopathies (and ancestry) affect HbA1c, glucose-based testing (FPG/OGTT) should be used when HbA1c reliability is in question.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS: "some clinical conditions (e.g., hemoglobinopathies) and ancestry may influence the HbA1c."

items.assertion: Aggressively lowering glucose can improve a type 2 diabetic patient's own insulin secretion.
items.reason: Chronic hyperglycemia impairs islet function through 'glucose toxicity,' which is partly reversible.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Glucose toxicity reversibly impairs beta-cell function, so improved glycemic control often improves endogenous insulin secretion — a rationale for prompt glucose lowering.
items.reference: IMPAIRED INSULIN SECRETION: "chronic hyperglycemia paradoxically impairs islet function ('glucose toxicity')... Improvement in glycemic control is often associated with improved islet function."

items.assertion: Diabetes detected at an 8-week prenatal visit should be classified as preexisting diabetes rather than gestational diabetes.
items.reason: Gestational diabetes, by definition, develops in the second or third trimester due to pregnancy-related insulin resistance.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because GDM arises in the 2nd/3rd trimester, first-trimester (8-week) diabetes-range hyperglycemia is classified as preexisting (pregestational) diabetes.
items.reference: GESTATIONAL DM: "diabetes diagnosed within the first trimester be classified as preexisting pregestational diabetes rather than GDM"; GDM is "glucose intolerance developing during the second or third trimester."

items.assertion: C-peptide can help distinguish surreptitious insulin injection from an insulinoma in a patient with hypoglycemia.
items.reason: C-peptide is co-secreted with endogenous insulin but is absent from pharmaceutical insulin preparations.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Endogenous insulin secretion releases C-peptide; injected (exogenous) insulin does not contain C-peptide, so a high insulin with low C-peptide indicates exogenous insulin, while both elevated suggests endogenous hypersecretion (e.g., insulinoma).
items.reference: INSULIN BIOSYNTHESIS: C-peptide "is a useful marker of insulin secretion and allows discrimination of endogenous and exogenous sources of insulin in the evaluation of hypoglycemia."

items.assertion: A negative family history does not exclude type 1 diabetes in a patient.
items.reason: More than 80% of individuals with type 1 diabetes have no affected first-degree relative.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Since >80% of people with type 1 DM have no affected relative (and twin concordance is only 30–70%), absence of family history cannot rule out the diagnosis.
items.reference: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS: "the majority of individuals with type 1 DM (>80%) do not have a relative with this disorder."
